preamble: The development of nasalized vowels from the loss of a preceding or following nasal consonant is a common historical phonetic shift, often referred to as compensatory nasalization. This process involves the nasalization of an oral vowel due to the influence of an adjacent nasal consonant, which is subsequently lost, leaving the vowel with its acquired nasal quality. This transformation has played a significant role in the phonological evolution of many languages, notably in the Romance family.
phoneticEffects: Compensatory nasalization, vowel nasalization, nasal consonant deletion

examples.note: The Latin sequence of vowel + nasal consonant (/an/) led to the nasalization of the vowel and the subsequent loss of the nasal consonant, resulting in the nasalized vowel /ɛ̃/.

sources: Strong, H. A., & Kitchin, A. W. (1901). A Historical Reader of Early French: Containing Passages Illustrative of the Growth of the French Language from the Earliest Times to the End of the Fifteenth Century. Clarendon Press.
